text: This video will illustrate a robotic left apical trisegmentectomy in a young patient with an adenocarcinoma of the left upper lobe.
Dissection is started by mobilizing the inferior pulmonary ligament to the level of the inferior pulmonary vein.
No level nine lymph nodes are seen.
We go ahead and then turn the lung medially to expose the posterior aspect of the hilum.
And when doing this, we're going to open up that posterior pleura.
That pearly white structure that's coming into view there is the pulmonary artery.
And we're going to just stay in the pulmonary artery and dissect that all the way up towards the aortic arch.
Once that's done, the apex of the lung is folded down towards the diaphragm and dissection is carried out by tissue on the AP window.
Again, we're staying close onto the pulmonary artery.
We next turn the lung laterally to expose the medial aspect or the anterior aspect of the hilum.
And we'll go ahead and expose the superior pulmonary vein going to the left upper lobe.
Here you can see the lingual vein at the bottom of the screen there and the vein going to the trisegment at the top of the screen.
There's a lymph node right at the end, the bifurcation of the veins.
That's also been removed.
And once we do this, we're gonna slowly come around the vein here.
Once we're fully around the vein, the vascular load of the stapler is used and the vein is then transected.
The lingular vein again is at the bottom of the screen and it's been spared.
Again, some more lymph nodes are seen and these are all removed.

It's important that during these segmentectomies, these intersegmental lymph nodes that we come across are all removed so that makes the dissection easier and helps get around the structures easier and accommodate the staplers.
And here's another lymph node that we're taking out.
This here is likely the truncus branch to the left upper lobe.
And it's going to be dissected out and then transected to the vascular node.
Here again, another branch to the left upper lobe there.
We're going to try to approach that posteriorly to see if there's a lymph node that needs to be removed to help us better facilitate getting around that branch.
It doesn't seem that there's a lymph node there, so we're going to go ahead and try to get a vascular load of that stapler to come across that pulmonary artery branch, and then it's transected.
Once that's done, we're gonna go ahead and dissect out the bronchus to the trisegment.
And here you can see that highlighted pretty easily is the trisegment bronchus.
We're next gonna go into the fissure to complete the posterior aspect of this fissure.
The pulmonary artery is seen
And that section is just, again, carried out right on top of that pulmonary artery.
We're going to extend it posteriorly towards the back of that fissure to slowly complete it.
I'm using a cigar here to do some blunt dissection onto that pulmonary artery from the posterior aspect of it to see if I can get that cigar to go
across into the fissure from the other side.
This is a trisegmental branch, the airway, and we're gonna go ahead and transect it with the vascular or the stapler there.
Here again, we're back in that posterior aspect of the PA, posterior aspect of the fissure.
We're gonna put that cigar in there again to see if we can deliver it from the fissural side.
There's some gentle spreading.
I'm starting to see that cigar coming into view there, and there we're just gonna deliver it into the fissure.

And in doing this, I can go ahead and complete that posterior aspect of the fissure as long as I stay on top of the cigar, I should be okay in terms of protecting the pulmonary artery or any small branches.
It's a very safe way to go ahead and take the fissure.
Once that's done, we've taken all the segments to the apical triseg here.
We use Firefly to essentially demarcate the segmental fissure.
The part that's lighting up is the lingular fissure, sorry, the lingular segment.
And the part that's not lighting up is the trisegment.
We're going to go ahead and mark that with our bipolar grasper here.
and we'll use that as our staple line.
Multiple green loads of the stapler are used to help us complete the fissure.
And as we're coming along the fissure in this plane here, I'm making sure that I'm incorporating the airway, the artery and the vein within the specimen side for margins.
Sometimes when you get to this portion of the segment, the tissue is too thick for the green loads and you may have to use a black load to come across.
But it seems that in this case, the green loads are working just fine.
This completes the segmentectomy.
The patient did very well postoperatively and was sent home the next day.
